EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably hot temperatures occurred in advance of a slow moving cold front. High temperatures across portions of west-central and Downeast Maine ranged from around 90 to the mid 90s...with dewpoints of around 70 to the lower 70s. Apparent temperatures generally ranged from the upper 90s to around 105.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Apparent temperatures ranged from the upper 90s to around 105 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across Downeast areas through the late morning and early afternoon hours of the 30th in advance of an approaching cold front. Some of the thunderstorms became severe...producing damaging winds and large hail. The storms crossed Downeast areas...including the coast...through the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was toppled on to Leighton Point Road by wind gusts estimated at up to 60 mph. The time is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving training thunderstorms developed across the Downeast coast during the early morning hours of the 9th along a cold front. The thunderstorms produced very heavy rainfall and flash flooding along portions of the Downeast coast. Rainfall totals of 2.00 to 4.00 inches were common...with local totals in excess of 5.00 inches. The resultant flash flooding damaged many roads...culverts and bridges to varying extents. Road closures were common with large portions of several roads entirely washed out or heavily damaged.  The road damage was particularly extensive in the towns of Machias...Winter Harbor and Prospect Harbor. Some residents were isolated due to washed out roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall totals generally ranged from 2.00 to 4.00 inches...with local totals in excess of 5.00 inches. The heavy rains produced flash flooding. Numerous roads were closed or damaged to varying extents due to flooding and washouts. Damage to bridges and culverts also occurred. Numerous carriage roads and hiking trails were damaged in Acadia National Park on Mount Desert Island.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were toppled on to powerlines by wind gusts estimated at up to 60 mph. The time is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Fast moving low pressure crossed northern Maine from the late evening of the 24th through the early morning hours of the 25th. The storm produced heavy snow across northern Somerset county. Snow began during the evening of the 24th and persisted into the early morning hours of the 25th. Warning criteria snow accumulations occurred during the early morning hours of the 25th. Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 7 to 9 inches. Lesser snow totals occurred across the remainder of northern Maine.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 7 to 9 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions generally worsened across the region during June. Severe drought conditions developed late in the month across portions of central and southern Piscataquis county along with southern Penobscot county. June rainfall for Bangor was only 0.97 inches...the second driest June on record. This was also the 4th warmest June on record for Bangor. Yearly rainfall totals for Bangor at the end of June were around 4.50 inches below normal. Near normal ground moisture and river levels existed at the start of the growing season in early May. Due to the dry conditions...water levels on the Penobscot and Piscataquis Rivers and tributaries had fallen to well below normal levels by late June. Lakes and reservoirs were also running at below normal levels at months end. Still being early in the growing season...agricultural impacts had been limited up to this point. The dry conditions also contributed to a relatively higher than normal number of wildfires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions worsened during June. Severe drought developed late month...then persisted through the remainder of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approached during the 21st...while an upper level disturbance crossed the region. Thunderstorms developed across northern Maine during the afternoon. Some of the thunderstorms became severe producing damaging winds. Microbursts occurred at two locations producing extremely strong winds. The first microburst occurred in an area from Castle Hill... to Crouseville...to southwest Caribou. Microburst winds in this area were estimated at 90 to 100 mph. The microburst originated near Castle Hill where around three dozen trees were snapped or toppled along with damage to outdoor equipment. Tree damage also occurred in the Crouseville and Washburn areas. The metal roof of a barn was damaged in southwest Caribou where the microburst diminished. A second microburst occured in Limestone where winds were estimated at 100 to 110 mph. The winds almost completely destroyed one barn...while the roof was torn from a second barn. Large trees were also snapped or toppled.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were toppled by wind gusts estimated at up to 60 mph. The time is estimated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced a microburst in the Limestone area. One barn was completely destroyed. Another barn has its roof torn off. Large trees were also snapped or toppled along the nearly 5 mile path of the microburst which ended aboubt a half mile north of Limestone. One uprooted tree fell on to the roof of a house. Maximum wind speeds with the microburst were estimated at 100 to 110 mph. The maximum path width was around a half mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure tracked across the Gulf of Maine and Downeast Maine during the 2nd...exiting across the Maritimes during the 3rd. Snow developed during the late evening of the 1st across Downeast areas ...expanding across the remainder of the region through the early morning hours of the 2nd. Snow...heavy at times...then persisted through the 2nd. The snow mixed with sleet and freezing rain at times...most extensive across central and Downeast areas. Warning criteria accumulations occurred through the morning and afternoon of the 2nd. Precipitation then ended from the late evening hours of the 2nd through the early morning hours of the 3rd. Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 9 to 15 inches across northern areas. Wind gusts of 30 to 35 mph occurred across northern areas producing areas of blowing and drifting snow. Across central portions of the region.. storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 7 to 11 inches along with up to 1 to 2 inches of sleet. Snow accumulations Downeast varied dependent on the extent of mixing with sleet and freezing rain. Storm total snow accumulations across interior Downeast areas and coastal portions of Washington County generally ranged from 6 to 10 inches...with local totals up to around 12 inches. Storm total snow accumulations across coastal Hancock county generally ranged from 3 to 6 inches...where a more extensive mixture with sleet occurred. Sleet accumulations across interior and coastal Downeast areas ranged up to 2 to 3 inches. Wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph occurred across Downeast areas with the strongest winds along the Downeast coast. Ice accumulations of 1/10 to 2/10 of an inch also occurred across much of the region.|The strong onshore flow and large waves led to coastal flooding across portions of coastal Hancock county around the time of high tide during the afternoon of the 2nd. Seawall Road was closed for a time due to water and debris covering the road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 7 to 10 inches...along with around an inch of sleet. Ice accumulations of 1/10 to 2/10 of an inch also occurred. Winds gusted to 30 to 35 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving intensifying low tracked across the Gulf of Maine...close to the Downeast coast...to New Brunswick from the 15th to the 16th. Snow expanded northeast across the region through the early morning hours of the 16th...persisting into the afternoon. Bands of heavy snow occurred across northern portions of the region...with snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour at times. Warning criteria snow and sleet accumulations occurred during the early afternoon hours. Precipitation then diminished through the mid and late afternoon. Storm total snow accumulations across northern portions of the region generally ranged from 6 to 10 inches. Across central portions of the region...snow accumulations ranged from 3 to 6 inches along with 1 to 2 inches of sleet. Across interior Downeast areas...snow accumulations ranged from 2 to 5 inches along with 1 to 3 inches of sleet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 5 to 8 inches.

Bands of heavy snow occurred across northern portions of the region...with snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour at times. Warning criteria snow and sleet accumulations occurred during the early afternoon hours. Precipitation then diminished through the mid and late afternoon. Storm total snow accumulations across northern portions of the region generally ranged from 6 to 10 inches. Across central portions of the region...snow accumulations ranged from 3 to 6 inches along with 1 to 2 inches of sleet. Across interior Downeast areas...snow accumulations ranged from 2 to 5 inches along with 1 to 3 inches of sleet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 7 to 10 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure tracked across the Gulf of Maine and Downeast Maine during the 2nd...exiting across the Maritimes during the 3rd. Snow developed during the late evening of the 1st across Downeast areas ...expanding across the remainder of the region through the early morning hours of the 2nd. Snow...heavy at times...then persisted through the 2nd. The snow mixed with sleet and freezing rain at times...most extensive across central and Downeast areas. Warning criteria accumulations occurred through the morning and afternoon of the 2nd. Precipitation then ended from the late evening hours of the 2nd through the early morning hours of the 3rd. Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 9 to 15 inches across northern areas. Wind gusts of 30 to 35 mph occurred across northern areas producing areas of blowing and drifting snow. Across central portions of the region.. storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 7 to 11 inches along with up to 1 to 2 inches of sleet. Snow accumulations Downeast varied dependent on the extent of mixing with sleet and freezing rain. Storm total snow accumulations across interior Downeast areas and coastal portions of Washington County generally ranged from 6 to 10 inches...with local totals up to around 12 inches. Storm total snow accumulations across coastal Hancock county generally ranged from 3 to 6 inches...where a more extensive mixture with sleet occurred. Sleet accumulations across interior and coastal Downeast areas ranged up to 2 to 3 inches. Wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph occurred across Downeast areas with the strongest winds along the Downeast coast. Ice accumulations of 1/10 to 2/10 of an inch also occurred across much of the region.|The strong onshore flow and large waves led to coastal flooding across portions of coastal Hancock county around the time of high tide during the afternoon of the 2nd. Seawall Road was closed for a time due to water and debris covering the road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 7 to 10 inches. Ice accumulations of 1/10 to 2/10 of an inch also occurred.  Winds gusted to 30 to 35 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late season severe weather outbreak occurred across Maine as a cold front swept across the state during the afternoon peak heating.  A very moist and unstable airmass was in place across the region with highs reaching 80 degrees and dewpoints into the 60s.  This allowed for sufficient CAPE values to support damaging winds gusts and large hail as the cold front moved across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damaging outflow wind gusts from a severe thunderstorm downed multiple trees across the Lewiston/Auburn Area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving low pressure system moved across southern New England late Thursday night through Friday before entering into the Gulf of Maine by Saturday. Rain and snow started first across the Western Maine Mountains early Friday morning on the 16th before quickly changed over to all snow across higher elevations above 1000ft. The snow became moderate at times by midday Friday as banding developed on the northwest side of the deepening surface low. Snow began to taper off and become showery in nature as the system filled and weakened as it pulled further off shore. Key Impacts: transportation delays, heavy wet snow leading to minor power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow moved into the area during the early morning hours on the 16th and continued into the evening. The majority of the snowfall accumulated through the afternoon hours as a mesoscale snowband with moderate snowfall rates moved into the Western Maine Mountains.  Due to the late season snowfall occurring during daylight hours, the snowfall struggled to accumulate in elevations below 800ft.  This led to a highly elevation dependent snowfall event with only a slushy inch across the lower elevation river valley floors and up to 8 in the mountains.  The heavy wet snow led to isolated power outages due to weighed down tree limbs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal trough and surface cold front pushed through Maine through the afternoon of June 19th.  An unstable atmosphere, combined with abundant shear allowed a line of storms to develop across Somerset County.  The line of storms gathered strength once they reached the Kennebec River Valley were they became severe.  Severe thunderstorms continued to track eastward through the Midcoast region through the afternoon hours.  One storm became an organized long track supercell that had a distinct wall cloud with an associated funnel cloud.  Tornado touchdown with this circulation was never found or confirmed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple photos and videos via social media showing low hanging rotating wall cloud and funnel cloud just north of Hope, ME.  Feature was associated with a well organized super cell, no confirmation of a tornado was found with this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure slowly tracked northeast across the region from late on the 21st into the 22nd before exiting across the Maritimes. Enough cold air existed north and west of the low track to support snow across northern Maine. Snow developed during the morning of the 21st. The snow diminished and transitioned to rain across northeast areas through the morning into the afternoon of the 21st. However, colder air persisted across northwest areas through the 22nd into the early morning hours of the 23rd allowing for an extended snow event with the upper low. Storm total snow accumulations across northeast areas generally ranged from 2 to 6 inches...with 6 to 10 inches across northwest areas. The greatest totals generally occurred across the higher terrain of the Saint John Valley and along the northwest Aroostook county border with Quebec province.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 3 to 6 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance and associated area of developing low pressure across Southern New England helped trigger thunderstorms across Western Maine. An unstable airmass caused by an advancing cold pool aloft and increasing shear allowed for storms to be come strong with an isolated severe thunderstorm. Damaging winds and small hail were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm wind gusts downed multiple trees on Greenwood Rd. south of Greenwood, Maine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid airmass with heat index values well into the 90s was firmly in place when an upper level trough and associated cold front begin to move into western Maine on the afternoon of June 30th.  Storms began to develop and quickly became severe due to very unstable conditions being fueled by the near record heat and humidity.  The storms traveled from west to east straight to the coast through the afternoon hours, leaving in their wake downed trees and power outages.  A particular damaging storm impacted the Belgrade Lakes region with 100s of down trees and property damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm passed through the Belgrade Lakes region on Wednesday afternoon, producing a wet microburst. Estimated wind speeds varied from 60 mph up to 90 mph. There was a concentrated damage swath about 2 miles long beginning along the eastern shore of long pond just north of Belgrade Village. The damage ended on Abena Shore Rd. to the southeast of Belgrade Village.  Approximately 300 trees were uprooted and snapped with the heaviest pockets of damage immediately on the eastern shorelines of Long Pond and Hersom Point on great pond.  In addition to the tree damage, approximately 6 buildings were partially damaged due to trees falling on them. An additional building had shingle damage directly caused by the wind.  Two vehicles were damaged when trees fell on them. One fatality occurred when a tree fell on a pedestrian in a parked vehicle. The tree damage was extensive with many old growth white pines, oaks and willows being uprooted and or snapped. Power outages were extensive in the area with many snapped poles and downed lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An ice jam developed on the Aroostook River in the vicinity of Crouseville during the early morning hours of the 29th. River levels rose in the vicinity of the ice jam resulting in the closure of Route 164 in the vicinity of Crouseville. The road remained closed until the late morning of the 30th when the ice jam released. River levels fell with the release of the ice jam allowing the road to re-open.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice jam on the Aroostook River in the vicinity of Crouseville led to the closure of Route 164 from the early morning hours of the 29th through the late morning of the 30th. The ice jam released during the late morning of the 30th allowing river levels to fall and allowing the road to re-open.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An ice jam developed on the Aroostook River during the afternoon of the 27th in the vicinity of Grimes Road...leading to the closure of the road. Fluctuating river levels in the vicinity of the ice jam caused the road to be opened and re-closed several times from the 27th into the 30th. The ice jam released during the afternoon of the 30th allowing river levels to fall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice jam on the Aroostook River in the vicinity of the Grimes Road led to fluctuating river levels. This led to the occasional closure of the road starting on the afternoon of the 27th and persisting into the afternoon of the 30th when the ice jam released.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An ice jam developed on the Aroostook River in the vicinity of Fort Fairfield during the 1st causing rising river levels. The rising river levels eventually led to the closure of Riverside Road and Russell Road during the evening of the 1st. The roads remained closed into the evening of the 4th when the ice jam released allowing river levels to fall and allowing the roads to re-open.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice jam on the Aroostook River in the vicinity of Fort Fairfield led to rising river levels and the closure of Riverside Road and Russell Road. The roads were closed from the evening of the 1st into the evening of the 4th when the ice jam released allowing river levels to fall and allowing the roads to re-open.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense low pressure curled northeast through the Bay of Fundy on the 1st. Early on the 2nd an arctic cold front wrapped into the circulation and blasted through northern New England. Strong cold air advection led to a well mixed boundary layer and strong wind gusts across much of the forecast area. A core of 60 to 70 knot winds near ridge top level with a strong inversion above resulted in a favorable set up for accelerating downslope winds southeast of the higher terrain. The strongest wind gusts lasted from morning until early afternoon. Key Impacts: Strong sustained winds, damaging wind gusts, numerous power outages, minor structural damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds began to sharply increase early in the morning on the 2nd. While strong wind gusts were common over a large portion of the area, damaging wind gusts were most likely in the lee of the taller mountains and ridges. Several wind gusts in excess of 50 knots were measured on local mesonets, including two separate wind gusts of 63 knots south of the Bigelow Mountains. About 25 percent of Franklin County customers lost power during the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracking west of Maine drew an occluded front across the region during the afternoon of the 1st...with a strong cold front following that evening. Snow accumulations of 2 to 5 inches occurred across eastern portions of Aroostook county and northern Penobscot county. The low intensified across the Gulf of Saint Lawrence during the 2nd. The tight pressure gradient between the intensifying low and high pressure to the west led to strong northwest winds. Winds were sustained at 20 to 30 mph...with gusts up to 50 mph. These winds combined with the newly fallen snow to create extensive blowing and drifting snow...resulting in very hazardous driving conditions. White-out conditions along with snow being drifted across roads led to accidents and road closures through the morning into the evening of the 2nd. The worst conditions generally occurred in open areas exposed to the northwest winds. Additional road crews had to be called in from other parts of the state to help the local crews keep roads open. Warming shelters were opened in various areas to help stranded motorists who had to abandon their vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds combined with newly fallen snow to produce extensive blowing and drifting snow. The blowing and drifting snow produced very hazardous driving conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late season severe weather outbreak occurred across Maine as a cold front swept across the state during the afternoon peak heating.  A very moist and unstable airmass was in place across the region with highs reaching 80 degrees and dewpoints into the 60s.  This allowed for sufficient CAPE values to support damaging winds gusts and large hail as the cold front moved across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damaging outflow wind gusts from a severe thunderstorm downed trees, wires and a pole in Shapleigh.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions continued through September across portions of western Maine. Drought conditions had developed in May 2021, with severe drought developing by mid-June. September brought above normal rainfall for all but northwestern Maine where moderate and severe drought conditions continued. A widespread soaking rain brought an inch to most areas between the 24th and 26th, with locally higher amounts upwards of 6 inches over the Midcoast and central Maine. This beneficial rainfall when combined with the end of the growing season lowered water demands for much of the region.  The U.S. drought monitor highlighted these positive trends with a slight reduction in moderate drought areas across central Maine.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The moderate (D1) drought conditions across Maine were reduced to a (D0) across portions of southern Oxford, Franklin, and Somerset Counties. Severe drought conditions remain unchanged for the central and northern portions of those counties as they received less rainfall. Between the beginning and end of September, the severe drought (D2) was reduced from 13.6% of the region to 7.5%. The cumulative area in drought (D0-D2) was reduced from 51% to 32% for the same period.  Rainfall deficits continued for the headwaters of the Penobscot, Kennebec, and Androscoggin Rivers, where D0 to D2 conditions remain.  Year to date deficits continued for the drought stricken Lakes Region with reports of  6 to 12 inches below normal since January 1. Short term recharge was observed over portions of the Kennebeck and Penobscot headwaters where reservoirs returned to near normal levels. Northern and western Maine reported near record low groundwater levels based on the USGS well stations in the area. Dry wells were reported in northern counties, notably in Oxford, Franklin, and Somerset Counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest winds developed in the morning behind a departing cold front.  The surface low continued to deepen across the Canadian Maritimes to the northeast increasing the pressure gradient forcing.  The strong late March sun also allowed for mixing during the daylight hours which brought mixed a strong low level jet down to the surface.  Widespread 50 mph wind gusts occurred with some local gusts on the lee-side of the White and Western Maine Mountains gusting near 70 mph.  Scattered power outages were reported across Maine with approximately 60,000 outages.  One fatality was reported in Farmington due to a downed tree on their vehicle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated to be around 50 mph were impacting the region through the morning hours.  An eastern white pine tree snapped at approximately 8:53 AM EST at Knolton Corner Road in North Chesterville.  Large tree branches feel on a vehicle passing by with one of them hitting the roof of the driver side.  The driver died of injuries sustained by the impact of the tree branch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong west to northwest winds occurred during the 29th. Winds were generally sustained at 20 to 30 mph...with gusts of 45 to 55 mph. However...a wind gust of 60 mph was reported by the Greenville ASOS during the early afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Greenville ASOS reported a 60 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense low pressure curled northeast through the Bay of Fundy on the 1st. Early on the 2nd an arctic cold front wrapped into the circulation and blasted through northern New England. Strong cold air advection led to a well mixed boundary layer and strong wind gusts across much of the forecast area. A core of 60 to 70 knot winds near ridge top level with a strong inversion above resulted in a favorable set up for accelerating downslope winds southeast of the higher terrain. The strongest wind gusts lasted from morning until early afternoon. Key Impacts: Strong sustained winds, damaging wind gusts, numerous power outages, minor structural damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds began to sharply increase early in the morning on the 2nd. While strong wind gusts were common over a large portion of the area, damaging wind gusts were most likely in the lee of the taller mountains and ridges. About 30 percent of Oxford County customers lost power during the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure intensified as it entered interior New England on January sixteenth with a 991mb surface low being observed. This produced storm force easterly wind gusts over the outer waters of the Gulf of Maine and 16 foot nearshore waves with a period of 8 seconds.  These large waves and storm surge coincided at the time of high tide leading to coastal flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The astronomical tide in Portland of 10.2 feet MLLW combined with a 2.0 foot storm surge to produce a 12.2 foot MLLW storm tide during the early afternoon hours. Flood stage is 12.0 feet at the Portland gage. The tide remained above flood stage for a full 90 minutes.  Significant splash-over occurred along Webhennet Drive in Wells as waves inundated and partially closed the road.  In addition, water, sand and boulders closed Main, Eastern and Riverside Avenues in the Camp Ellis area of Saco.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The astronomical tide in Portland of 10.2 feet MLLW combined with a 2.0 foot storm surge to produce a 12.2 foot MLLW storm tide during the early afternoon hours. Flood stage is 12.0 feet at the Portland gage. The tide remained above flood stage for a full 90 minutes.  Flooding occurred up to six inches deep on Somerset St. with water close to a foot deep at Marginal Way in Portland with the lowest spots on the roadway covered by two to three feet of water. The parking lots of local restaurants were flooded in Pine Point and along the upper portion of the marsh in Scarborough.  In addition, waves crashed onto lawns at Peebles Cove in Cape Elizabeth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved through the Ohio River Valley late on the 1st and tracked into New York early on the 2nd. A secondary low pressure began to develop early on the 2nd in the vicinity of Chesapeake Bay. By sunrise the secondary low pressure had become dominant and moved across Buzzards Bay and through the Gulf of Maine. The initial wave of precipitation moved in with strong warm air advection aloft late on the evening of the 1st. Early in the morning on the 2nd above freezing air and drying out of the column allowed some of that precipitation to change over to sleet, freezing rain, and rain. As the storm deepened in the Gulf of Maine, mid levels cooled and precipitation flipped back to snow for several hours before ending around midday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow moved into the western Maine mountains just around midnight on the 2nd. Light to moderate snow continued into the early morning hours of the 2nd before tapering off. Precipitation was predominantly snow but some sleet or freezing rain mixed in at times. Towards mid-morning steady snow moved back into the area and continued until ending as some wraparound light snow. Snow totals ranged from 6 to 10 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the 15th low pressure occluded over the Great Lakes and secondary cyclogenesis began near the triple point off the Mid Atlantic coast. Secondary low pressure rapidly deepened into the 16th. This strong low pressure developed an equally strong moisture transport which impinged upon the higher terrain with upslope enhancement. A very marginal air mass for mid January meant that snowfall totals were highly dependent on elevation in order for temperatures to be cold enough for all or mostly snow. Warmer temperatures and downsloping meant that valley locations had significantly less snow than higher elevations. Precipitation was heavy at times but generally fell over a 6 to 10 hour period and limited snowfall totals to around a foot at most.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began on the morning of the 16th and continued into the mid afternoon hours. A mild mid January air mass resulted in a strong elevation dependence to snowfall amounts. Snowfall around 12 inches occurred in the higher elevations of the Mahoosuc Mountains with totals closer to 5 inches in the valleys.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving intensifying low tracked across the Gulf Of Maine during the 2nd. Snow developed across the region during the early morning hours of the 2nd...then persisted through much of the afternoon. The snow was heavy at times in bands through the morning into the afternoon across interior Downeast areas and portions of the central highlands...with snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour at times. The heaviest snow was concentrated in an area from Greenville...to Houlton...to Calais...to Bangor. Warning criteria snow accumulations occurred through the late morning into the afternoon. The snow diminished through the late afternoon hours. Storm total snow accumulations in the area of heaviest snow ranged from 6 to 13 inches. Lesser snow fell across northern areas...further from the track. Mixed precipitation limited snow totals along the Downeast coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 8 to 10 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved into the Ohio Valley on the 31st, where it began to encounter resistance from high pressure anchored over James Bay. As the upper trough continued east, secondary low pressure began to develop along the warm front near the Outer Banks. This new low pressure center deepened and quickly occluded south of Long Island by midday on the 1st. It meandered south of Long Island into the morning of the 2nd before finally moving northeastward into the Gulf of Maine during the latter half of the day. During the occlusion phase a strong, pivoting mesoscale band of heavy snow impacted parts of the Mid Atlantic, while the northeastern extension of the band remained rather progressive lifting through New England. The heavy snow ended early on the 2nd but snow and significant amounts of sleet continued through the day for much of western Maine. Precipitation tapered off late on the 2nd. Key Impacts: transportation delays, traffic accidents, heavy snow, heavy sleet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow moved into the area just after midnight and continued through the morning hours on the 2nd. The bulk of the accumulation came with moderate to heavy snow in the mesoscale snow band as it moved north through the western Maine mountains. Snow continued into the evening in waves as additional moisture was pulled inland from the Gulf of Maine and forced over the higher terrain. Snowfall totals ranged from 8 to 14 inches, with the highest amounts in the Bigelow Mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure rapidly intensified down to 988mb as it passed off the New England coast into the Gulf of Maine during the day on February 2nd.  This produced storm force northeastly winds over the coastal waters that led to 25 foot nearshore waves.  In addition, a storm surge up to 2 feet was also recorded at tidal gauges.  The combination of the high tide, surge and large breaking waves caused coastal flooding across York County Maine.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The astronomical tide in Portland of 10ft MLLW combined with a storm surge of 1.9ft to bring the storm tide water level up to 11.9ft, which was just below flood stage at the Portland tide gage.  The significant near shear waves of up to 25ft combined with the high tide to cause wave run-up along the coast.  Battering waves caused splash-over and over-wash of coastal roads in Saco, which led to flooding and a stranded truck on Riverside Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance crossing Downeast Maine supported the development of thunderstorms during the afternoon of the 16th. An isolated severe thunderstorm produced large hail in Hancock county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The time is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably hot and humid airmass moved into the region allowing for instability values more typical for summer.  In addition, a pre-frontal trough approached Maine ahead of the main surface cold front located to the west in Quebec Province. A strong jet streak aloft allowed for strong 0-6sm shear profiles.  These ingredients led to organized linear convection by the afternoon hours with multiple straight line wind damage across the southwest corner of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorms downed trees and wires on Ossipee Hill Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Significant dryness due to lack of rainfall saw short term drought conditions deteriorating in June from Moderate to Severe Drought according to the U.S. Drought Monitor.  By the end of June, the U.S. Drought Monitor classified most of New Hampshire and Western Maine as observing drought conditions. A moderate drought level (D1) or higher was in place for 70% of Maine and 58% New Hampshire. Severe drought (D2) conditions were designated for 20% of Maine over portions of Oxford, Franklin, and Somerset counties, extending westward into a portion of Coos County in New Hampshire. Southern portions of New Hampshire along the Massachusetts border were the only areas currently not designated in drought at the time. ||The drought developed as a result of below normal precipitation this spring followed by a warm and dry start to the summer season.  Groundwater levels didn't get the typical recharge from winter snowmelt or spring rains, so the deterioration of soil moisture in June led to a rapid drop in streamflows, reservoir levels, and well readings. Areas designated as a moderate drought or worse were running 6 inches or more below normal rainfall for the year.  Much of the area within D1 or worse drought designation had observed 25 to 50% of their normal precipitation over between May and June, while areas that were abnormally dry (D0) observed 70% of normal precipitation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The state of Maine activated its Drought Task Force. Several towns instituted voluntary water restrictions. Hydro operators balanced needs of lake levels, hydroelectric power generation needs, and the discharges to maintain stream flow needs downstream. In Maine, the South Berwick Water District has issued emergency mandatory water use restrictions. Most of the reported  water quantity issues are from southern and coastal areas. Farmers without irrigation experienced stressed vegetation from a lack of rain and near record temperatures in June. First-cut hay crop was near normal, but subsequent hay growth was slowed. Most streamflow stations in central and western Maine with 30+ years of record were at the lowest they have ever been for that time of year, even compared to the 2020 drought.

Areas designated as a moderate drought or worse were running 6 inches or more below normal rainfall for the year.  Much of the area within D1 or worse drought designation had observed 25 to 50% of their normal precipitation over between May and June, while areas that were abnormally dry (D0) observed 70% of normal precipitation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The state of Maine activated its Drought Task Force. Several towns instituted voluntary water restrictions. Hydro operators balanced needs of lake levels, hydroelectric power generation needs, and the discharges to maintain stream flow needs downstream. In Maine, the South Berwick Water District has issued emergency mandatory water use restrictions. Most of the reported  water quantity issues are from southern and coastal areas. Farmers without irrigation experienced stressed vegetation from a lack of rain and near record temperatures in June. First-cut hay crop was near normal, but subsequent hay growth was slowed. Most streamflow stations in central and western Maine with 30+ years of record were at the lowest they have ever been for that time of year, even compared to the 2020 drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving training thunderstorms developed across the Downeast coast during the early morning hours of the 9th along a cold front. The thunderstorms produced very heavy rainfall and flash flooding along portions of the Downeast coast. Rainfall totals of 2.00 to 4.00 inches were common...with local totals in excess of 5.00 inches. The resultant flash flooding damaged many roads...culverts and bridges to varying extents. Road closures were common with large portions of several roads entirely washed out or heavily damaged.  The road damage was particularly extensive in the towns of Machias...Winter Harbor and Prospect Harbor. Some residents were isolated due to washed out roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall totals generally ranged from 2.00 to 4.00 inches...with local totals in excess of 5.00 inches. The heavy rains produced flash flooding. Numerous roads were closed or damaged to varying extents due to washouts. Damage to bridges and culverts also occurred. Extensive road damage occurred in the vicinity of Winter Harbor and across the Schoodic Peninsula. Main Street in Winter Harbor was flooded with water up to the entrances of some businesses. Washouts and road closures were reported on Birch Harbor Road...Summer Harbor Road. Due to road damage...there was no access to portions of Acadia National Park on the Schoodic Peninsula. Near Prospect Harbor...both Route 186 and Pond Road were closed due to flooding and washouts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably hot temperatures occurred in advance of a slow moving cold front. High temperatures across portions of west-central and Downeast Maine ranged from around 90 to the mid 90s...with dewpoints of around 70 to the lower 70s. Apparent temperatures generally ranged from the upper 90s to around 105.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Apparent temperatures ranged from the upper 90s to around 105 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure moved across Maine and sparked one severe thunderstorm that produced quarter size hail near Swanville, ME.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail fell in Swanville as a storm moved southeastward toward the coast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into a very moist air mass and sparked a few thunderstorms near the Maine coast during the early afternoon hours of the 20th.  A couple of these storms produced severe weather with isolated areas of wind damage being reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees downed in Saco, mainly on and near Route 1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The U.S. Drought Monitor classified Western Maine in drought. The drought developed as a result of below normal precipitation this spring followed by a warm and dry start to the summer season. The active weather pattern in July resulted in some areas receiving significant rainfall while others missed out. The foothills and mountains received the least amount of rainfall with these areas remaining in the severe drought introduced in late June. The coastal counties received three times their normal rainfall, effectively ending drought conditions for southern counties. The lakes and mountains regions saw less rainfall, averaging 75-125% of normal.  Severe drought across watershed headwaters kept most area rivers and reservoirs below normal levels. Precipitation in July was 150 to 300% in southern Maine. Northern Maine saw rainfall amounts around 75% of normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The July 1 issuance of the U.S. Drought Monitor had 100% of Maine a drought level of D0 or greater. A moderate drought level (D1) or higher was in place for 70% of Maine. Severe drought (D2) conditions were designated for 20% of Maine over portions of Oxford, Franklin, and Somerset counties, extending westward into a portion of Coos County in New Hampshire.  Dry wells were reported in northern counties, notably in Oxford and Franklin Counties. In both Maine and New Hampshire several towns instituted voluntary water restrictions, due primarily from below normal groundwater levels. Hydro operators are monitoring conditions and balancing needs of lake levels, hydroelectric power generation needs, and the discharges to maintain stream flow needs downstream.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system and it's associated cold air aloft moved over New England on the 23rd and resulted in sufficient instability and lift to produce a couple of severe thunderstorms.  The storms were short lived with brief periods of severe hail and wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail fell in East Livermore.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approached during the 21st...while an upper level disturbance crossed the region. Thunderstorms developed across northern Maine during the afternoon. Some of the thunderstorms became severe producing damaging winds. Microbursts occurred at two locations producing extremely strong winds. The first microburst occurred in an area from Castle Hill... to Crouseville...to southwest Caribou. Microburst winds in this area were estimated at 90 to 100 mph. The microburst originated near Castle Hill where around three dozen trees were snapped or toppled along with damage to outdoor equipment. Tree damage also occurred in the Crouseville and Washburn areas. The metal roof of a barn was damaged in southwest Caribou where the microburst diminished. A second microburst occured in Limestone where winds were estimated at 100 to 110 mph. The winds almost completely destroyed one barn...while the roof was torn from a second barn. Large trees were also snapped or toppled.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced a microburst which developed around Castle Hill and persisted through Crouseville and Washburn before ending in southwest Caribou. Around three dozen trees were snapped or toppled near Castle Hill...where some outdoor equipment was also damaged or destroyed. Tree damage was also observed in the Crouseville and Washburn areas. The metal roof of a barn was damaged in southwest Caribou before the microburst ended near Bailey Road. Maximum wind gusts with the microburst were estimated at 90 to 100 mph. The path length of the microburst was around 7 miles. The maximum path width was around 1 mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Post Tropical Storm Elsa moved along the Downeast coast during the afternoon and evening of the 9th bringing abundant tropical moisture and heavy rains to Downeast areas. Rainfall rates occasionally reached 1.00 to 2.00 inches per hour. Rainfall totals across Downeast areas through the afternoon and evening generally ranged from 2.50 to 4.00 inches...though local totals exceeded 5.00 inches. The heavy rains produced localized flash flooding in the Bangor and Brewer areas. Several roads were closed due to street flooding. Low antecedent river and stream levels helped mitigate the flood threat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains caused several road closures due to high water in the Bangor and Brewer areas. Canterbury and Church Roads were flooded in Brewer. An exit on Interstate 395 was closed due to a dirt washout across the road. In Bangor...Perry Road was closed at the Route 202 overpass due to street flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trof crossed the region during the afternoon and evening of the 23rd with a cold pool aloft. Diurnal heating and the cold pool aloft provided enough instability to support thunderstorm development  An isolated severe thunderstorm produced large hail in central Washington county along with 30 to 40 mph wind gusts. A plowable accumulation of pea size hail also occurred. Other thunderstorms also produced small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A plowable accumulation of pea size hail also occurred with 30 to 40 mph wind gusts.

A plowable accumulation of pea size hail also occurred. Other thunderstorms also produced small hail.

Severe drought conditions existed across portions of central and southern Piscataquis county...along with southern Penobscot county...at the start of July. Drought conditions began to improve across these areas early in the month. Several rain events...including rains from Post Tropical Storm Elsa...brought widespread rains during early July. Rainfall totals across central and southern Piscataquis county generally ranged from 1.00 to 3.00 inches from the remnants of Elsa. Greater rain totals occurred across southern Penobscot county where 3.00 to 5.00 inches occurred. With these rains, drought conditions improved from severe ( D2 ) to moderate ( D1 ). Even with the rains, river flows generally remained at below normal levels to varying degrees. Agricultural impacts remained limited, though some crops had started to require irrigation. Drought conditions in these areas then remained generally unchanged through the remainder of the month with several additional rain events and scattered showers and thunderstorms providing moisture. In fact, Bangor established an all time record for July rainfall with a monthly total of 7.67 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several rain events...including rains from Post Tropical Storm Elsa...led to improving drought conditions during early July. Drought conditions improved from Severe ( D2 ) at the start of the month...to Moderate ( D1 ). Moderate drought conditions then persisted through the remainder of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions continued across much of Maine in August 2021. During the month of July the coastal and southern counties received two to three times their normal rainfall, effectively ending drought conditions for those areas.  The Lakes and Mountains regions received less rainfall averaging 75-125% of normal, effectively keeping a status quo on the severe drought conditions going into August. Above normal temperatures in August increased evaporative demand and allowed drought conditions to persist in drought areas. The worst drought conditions were across the headwaters of the Androscoggin and Kennebec Rivers, with reservoir levels declining. Severe drought (D2) conditions were designated for 14% of Maine over portions of Oxford, Franklin, and Somerset Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall in August was below normal for most of the region, with above normal rainfall only over southern Maine where drought conditions no longer remain. For the areas in moderate to severe drought, rainfall was 40-70% of normal for the month. Temperatures meanwhile were running 4 to 6 degrees above normal, increasing evaporation. Rain from Tropical Cyclone Henri brought little if any relief to areas impacted by drought. Rainfall deficits continued for the headwaters of the Penobscot, Kennebec, and Androscoggin Rivers, where D2 conditions remain.  Northern and western Maine groundwater levels continued to recede based on the few well stations in the area. Dry wells have been reported in northern counties, notably in Oxford and Franklin Counties. The USDA Farm Service Agency has received a Secretarial Disaster Designation due to 8 weeks of continued severe drought conditions|in Maine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms formed just ahead of a cold front that moved toward the Midcoast of Maine during the afternoon hours on the 14th.  The line of storms stayed sub-severe except for one isolated storm that was strong enough to knock down a few trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm outflow winds downed a few trees in Cushing, one of which blocked traffic on River Road.  A nearby observation recorded a wind gust of 51 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed along a weak cold front late in the afternoon and early evening hours on the 12th. One storm became severe, knocking down trees and wires in Madison.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires downed in Madison via thunderstorm straight line winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Localized dense fog developed in the Kennebec River Valley on the early morning hours of August 12th.  A pedestrian was struck leading to a fatality (indirect).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Locally dense fog developed in the early morning hours in the Kennebec River|Valley.  The dense fog led to low visibilities which limited reaction time for motorists. At 0420 EST, the fog contributed to a single pedestrian being struck by a vehicle in the town of Monmouth, ME.  The accident claimed a life (indirect fatality).  The closest visibility sensor to the accident was at the Augusta Airport where the ASOS observation was reporting one quarter of mile around the time of the accident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions continued across much of Maine in August 2021. During the month of July the coastal and southern counties received two to three times their normal rainfall, effectively ending drought conditions for those areas.  The Lakes and Mountains regions received less rainfall averaging 75-125% of normal, effectively keeping a status quo on the severe drought conditions going into August. Above normal temperatures in August increased evaporative demand and allowed drought conditions to persist in drought areas. The worst drought conditions were across the headwaters of the Androscoggin and Kennebec Rivers, with reservoir levels declining. Severe drought (D2) conditions were designated for 14% of Maine over portions of Oxford, Franklin, and Somerset Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall in August was below normal for most of the region, with above normal rainfall only over southern Maine where drought conditions no longer remain. For the areas in moderate to severe drought, rainfall was 40-70% of normal for the month. Temperatures meanwhile were running 4 to 6 degrees above normal, increasing evaporation. Rain from Tropical Cyclone Henri brought little if any relief to areas impacted by drought. Rainfall deficits continued for the headwaters of the Penobscot, Kennebec, and Androscoggin Rivers, where D2 conditions remain.  Northern and western Maine groundwater levels continued to recede based on the few well stations in the area. Dry wells have been reported in northern counties, notably in Oxford and Franklin Counties. The USDA Farm Service Agency has received a Secretarial Disaster Designation due to 8 weeks of continued severe drought conditions|in Maine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intensifying surface low and upper low tracked along the Maine coast through the 26th...exiting across New Brunswick during the 27th. Snow began during the morning of the 26th across northern areas...persisting into the afternoon of the 27th across northeast areas. A rain/snow mix developed across central and Downeast areas during the 26th. Colder air allowed a transition to all snow through the afternoon and evening of the 26th...which also persisted into the afternoon of the 27th. Storm total snow accumulations across northern areas generally ranged from 4 to 8 inches...with locally greater totals along the northwest Maine border with Quebec. Snow accumulations across the rest of the region generally ranged from 2 to 5 inches...with local totals up to around 6 inches. This was the first measurable snow of the season for many Downeast areas. The snow did impact return Thanksgiving travel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 5 to 8 inches...with locally greater totals along the Quebec border.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal wave developed in the lower Ohio River Valley on the 18th and lifted northeastward towards Lake Erie. Cold air damming remained entrenched and secondary low pressure began developing farther east along the warm front, south of New England. This low slowly strengthened as it moved east northeastward just over the tip of Cape Cod. While snow overspread the entire area, the mid level warm front was able to advance into extreme southwestern Maine and changed precipitation over to sleet and freezing rain. As the storm continued east northeastward the snowfall struggled to Midcoast and west central Maine. The heaviest snowfall ran roughly west to east from the Lakes Region of New Hampshire to Portland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow overspread southwestern Maine in the afternoon on the 18th. Snow became moderate in the evening but otherwise remained light for the duration of the storm. After midnight on the 19th snow began to mix with sleet and freezing rain, mainly south of Route 109. Precipitation tapered off on the morning of the 19th. Storm total snowfall ranged from 7 inches near Lake Arrowhead to 4 inches near Berwick.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure tracked east of the region during the 15th with a trof extending back across northern Maine. Snow developed along the trof during the morning and persisted into the afternoon. Storm total snow accumulations across northern Aroostook county generally ranged from 2.0 to 3.0 inches...with 4.0 to 4.5 inches across higher terrain areas. This was the first measurable snow of the season for many areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 2.0 to 3.0 inches...with up to 4.0 to 4.5 inches across higher terrain areas.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Apparent temperatures ranged through the 90s to around 100.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly developing area of low pressure tracked across the Southern New England coast and directly over Western Maine on the evening of October 30th and into the early morning hours of October 31st.  Light rain fell during the day before moderate rain began overspreading the area by the evening. Periods of heavy rainfall occurred overnight, mainly within a 6 hour window.  Widespread reports of 2 to 4��� of rain were recorded in all areas except the Western Mountains.  Coastal York, Knox and Southern Waldo Counties received 4 to 6��� of rain with a few localized reports of 6.5+��� falling. The heavy rain and excessive runoff led to widespread nuisance flooding, with damage reported in the areas that were hit with the heaviest rainfall amounts.  River response on the preceding day led to minor river flooding, but the bulk of the flooding was due to flash flooding during the overnight hours.  Rivers were elevated during and immediately after the rain ended, but most remained within their banks. Only minor lowland flooding occurred from riverine flooding. Flooding of smaller and faster responding streams and creeks in the hardest hit areas did damage some area roads and bridges.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain with up to 6.5 causes excessive run off in the city of Biddeford.  The rapid urban run-off caused erosion on a retaining wall on the Saco River in downtown.  This caused the wall to collapse into the river with extensive damage to the riverfront park area.

Some of the hardest hit towns include Prospect, Swanville, Montville, Lincolnville, Northport and Monroe.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain with reports up to 6.5 inches fell in a short period of time lead to excessive run-off with numerous roads closed and damaged due to flooding in Knox County.  The towns that were hardest hit included Appleton, Camden, Hope, Rockland, Rockport and Thomaston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front approached during the 24th...then crossed the region from the 25th into the 26th. Abundant moisture was drawn north in advance of the front. An initial rain event occurred from the 24th into the 25th. This initial event generally produced 1.50 to 2.50 inches of rain...with local totals approaching 3.00 inches. A negatively tilting upper trough then helped draw moisture from an offshore low back along the cold frontal zone. This led to another rain event from the overnight hours of the 25th into the 26th. This second event produced another 1.50 to 2.50...with local totals up to around 3.00 inches...of rain in many of the same areas which received heavy rains from the first event. Flash flooding developed across portions of Hancock and southern Penobscot counties during the morning of the 26th. The flash flooding led to road closures along with several washouts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains led to flash flooding. The flash flooding prompted several road closures...with some roads also washed out. Road closures included County Wood Road in Newport and Main Road South in Hampden. Marion Drive and Old County Road in Hampden were washed out due to flash flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains led to flash flooding. The flash flooding prompted several road closures. Portions of both Routes 1 and 46 were closed in Bucksport. In Penobscot...Dunbar Road and New Road were closed. Several road closures were also reported in the vicinity of Orland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly developing area of low pressure tracked across the Southern New England coast and directly over Western Maine on the evening of October 30th and into the early morning hours of October 31st.  Light rain fell during the day before moderate rain began overspreading the area by the evening. Periods of heavy rainfall occurred overnight, mainly within a 6 hour window.  Widespread reports of 2 to 4��� of rain were recorded in all areas except the Western Mountains.  Coastal York, Knox and Southern Waldo Counties received 4 to 6��� of rain with a few localized reports of 6.5+��� falling. The heavy rain and excessive runoff led to widespread nuisance flooding, with damage reported in the areas that were hit with the heaviest rainfall amounts.  River response on the preceding day led to minor river flooding, but the bulk of the flooding was due to flash flooding during the overnight hours.  Rivers were elevated during and immediately after the rain ended, but most remained within their banks. Only minor lowland flooding occurred from riverine flooding. Flooding of smaller and faster responding streams and creeks in the hardest hit areas did damage some area roads and bridges.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain of 4 inches in the Portland area caused areas of urban flooding to include flooding at the intersection of Washington and Allen Ave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving training thunderstorms developed across the Downeast coast during the early morning hours of the 9th along a cold front. The thunderstorms produced very heavy rainfall and flash flooding along portions of the Downeast coast. Rainfall totals of 2.00 to 4.00 inches were common...with local totals in excess of 5.00 inches. The resultant flash flooding damaged many roads...culverts and bridges to varying extents. Road closures were common with large portions of several roads entirely washed out or heavily damaged.  The road damage was particularly extensive in the towns of Machias...Winter Harbor and Prospect Harbor. Some residents were isolated due to washed out roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall totals generally ranged from 2.00 to 4.00 inches...with local totals in excess of 5.00 inches. These heavy rains produced flash flooding. Numerous roads were closed or damaged to varying extents due to washouts. Damage to bridges and culverts also occurred. Some residents were left isolated due to the washouts. Many roads were impacted in the Machias area. Both roads leading into and out of Roque Bluffs were washed out between Roque Bluffs and Jonesboro. Great Cove Road and Old Route 1 were both closed due to washouts in the vicinity of Jonesboro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought classification of severe drought (D2) remained relatively unchanged in October for western Maine. Rainfall amounts were near to below normal for the month, with a dry period during the first two week. Above normal temperatures and below normal precipitation did lead to some minor expansion of D2 and D0 across the region.  However, the transition to fall reduced stress on the system with less evaporative and vegetation water losses.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Here is a breakdown of the U.S. Drought Monitor across western Maine from the issuance on November 2, 2021. Severe drought (D2) conditions were expanded slightly to the northeast across Somerset county, now totaling 9% areal extent in far western Maine. The moderate drought (D1) was reduced slightly as areas were upgraded to severe, with 4% coverage. There was also a reintroduction of D0 to southern Androscoggin and portions of Cumberland Counties. Though 35.24% of the state was classified as abnormally dry or drought conditions by area, approximately only 5.6% of Maine's population resided in the impacted area.|A monitoring well in Oxford County reported record lows for the month of October based on historical data (20-40 years). Rainfall in October was well below normal for most of the drought|stricken areas with deficits of 1 to 2 inches. Meanwhile, a late storm system on the 30th brought some heavy rainfall over the coastal plains where rainfall surpluses of 1 to 4 inches were observed. Annual deficits continued to be around a foot below normal for the areas in drought, specifically northern Oxford and Franklin Counties, and central Somerset.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure approached late on the 18th...tracking south of the region from the overnight hours of the 18th into the morning of the 19th. An inverted trof extended north across the region from the low. An upper level disturbance also crossed the region. These features all combined to produce snow across northern and eastern Maine. Snow initially developed across Downeast and west-central portions of the region during the evening of the 18th. Snow expanded across the rest of the region through the remainder of the evening into the early morning hours of the 19th. Snow persisted through the morning of the 19th...tapering to snow showers during the afternoon. Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 3 to 6 inches across much of the region...with 2 to 4 inches along the Downeast coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 2 to 4 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weakening area of low pressure approached from the west during the 22nd...while an ocean low intensified southeast of Nova Scotia. A trof extended from the ocean low...across eastern Maine...back to the approaching low. The trof helped focus an area of snow across the region. Snow began during the morning and persisted into the evening. Heavy snow occurred during the afternoon...with snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour at times.  Warning criteria snow accumulations occurred during the late afternoon and evening. Storm total snow accumulations across northeast areas generally ranged from 7 to 10 inches...with local totals to around 12 inches. Lesser snow totals occurred west and south of the band of heaviest snow. Gusty winds developed in the wake of the system producing areas of blowing snow from the overnight hours of the 22nd into the afternoon of the 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 4 to 6 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal wave developed in the lower Ohio River Valley on the 18th and lifted northeastward towards Lake Erie. Cold air damming remained entrenched and secondary low pressure began developing farther east along the warm front, south of New England. This low slowly strengthened as it moved east northeastward just over the tip of Cape Cod. While snow overspread the entire area, the mid level warm front was able to advance into extreme southwestern Maine and changed precipitation over to sleet and freezing rain. As the storm continued east northeastward the snowfall struggled to Midcoast and west central Maine. The heaviest snowfall ran roughly west to east from the Lakes Region of New Hampshire to Portland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow overspread the western Maine foothills by mid afternoon on the 18th. Snow became moderate in the evening but otherwise remained light for the duration of the storm. Snow tapered off in the morning on the 19th, finally ending as a couple of waves of light snow enhanced by upsloping winds over the mountains. Storm total snowfall ranged from 5 to 7 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure tracking northeast across Quebec province drew a cold front across the region through the evening of the 11th into the early morning hours of the 12th. A strong low level jet moved along the Downeast coast in advance of the cold front. Southerly/southwesterly winds were sustained between 25 and 35 mph with gusts of 50 to 60 mph. These winds brought down trees along portions of the Downeast coast. Trees falling on power lines contributed to power outages. The pressure gradient between the strong Quebec low and building high pressure then supported strong westerly winds during the 12th. Winds were sustained at 20 to 30 mph with gusts of 50 to 60 mph. The winds toppled trees across portions of Aroostook county and southern Piscataquis county. Trees falling on power lines contributed to power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Southerly/southwesterly winds were sustained between 25 and 35 mph with estimated gusts up to 60 mph. The winds snapped branches and toppled trees on to power lines leading to power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm and unstable airmass was in place across the region on the afternoon and evening of June 21st.  Shear was increasing by late afternoon as an upper level pre-front trough begin to reach the Quebec/Maine border region.  An isolated organized supercell thunderstorm developed north of Pittston Farms by late afternoon bringing damaging winds gusts and a possible wall cloud.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm wind gust downed numerous trees on the Northern Rd near Leadbetter Falls North of Pittston Farms.  A public picture near Pittston Farms showed a ragged looking wall cloud around the same time south of the tree damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal wave developed in the lower Ohio River Valley on the 18th and lifted northeastward towards Lake Erie. Cold air damming remained entrenched and secondary low pressure began developing farther east along the warm front, south of New England. This low slowly strengthened as it moved east northeastward just over the tip of Cape Cod. While snow overspread the entire area, the mid level warm front was able to advance into extreme southwestern Maine and changed precipitation over to sleet and freezing rain. As the storm continued east northeastward the snowfall struggled to Midcoast and west central Maine. The heaviest snowfall ran roughly west to east from the Lakes Region of New Hampshire to Portland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow overspread southwestern Maine by mid afternoon on the 18th. Snow became moderate in the evening but otherwise remained light for the duration of the storm. Snow tapered off in the morning on the 19th. Storm total snowfall ranged from 5 to 7 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow overspread southwestern Maine in the afternoon on the 18th. Snow became moderate in the evening but otherwise remained light for the duration of the storm. After midnight on the 19th snow began to mix with sleet and freezing rain, mainly south of Wells. Precipitation tapered off on the morning of the 19th. Storm total snowfall ranged from 7 inches near Biddeford to 2 inches at Ogunquit where sleet and freezing rain mixed in.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure tracking northeast across Quebec province drew a cold front across the region through the evening of the 11th into the early morning hours of the 12th. A strong low level jet moved along the Downeast coast in advance of the cold front. Southerly/southwesterly winds were sustained between 25 and 35 mph with gusts of 50 to 60 mph. These winds brought down trees along portions of the Downeast coast. Trees falling on power lines contributed to power outages. The pressure gradient between the strong Quebec low and building high pressure then supported strong westerly winds during the 12th. Winds were sustained at 20 to 30 mph with gusts of 50 to 60 mph. The winds toppled trees across portions of Aroostook county and southern Piscataquis county. Trees falling on power lines contributed to power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Westerly winds were sustained between 20 and 30 mph with estimated gusts up to 60 mph. The winds snapped branches and toppled trees on to power lines leading to power outages. Trees were reported down in Winterville and Guerette.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weakening area of low pressure approached from the west during the 22nd...while an ocean low intensified southeast of Nova Scotia. A trof extended from the ocean low...across eastern Maine...back to the approaching low. The trof helped focus an area of snow across the region. Snow began during the morning and persisted into the evening. Heavy snow occurred during the afternoon...with snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour at times.  Warning criteria snow accumulations occurred during the late afternoon and evening. Storm total snow accumulations across northeast areas generally ranged from 7 to 10 inches...with local totals to around 12 inches. Lesser snow totals occurred west and south of the band of heaviest snow. Gusty winds developed in the wake of the system producing areas of blowing snow from the overnight hours of the 22nd into the afternoon of the 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 7 to 10 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions continued through September across portions of western Maine. Drought conditions had developed in May 2021, with severe drought developing by mid-June. September brought above normal rainfall for all but northwestern Maine where moderate and severe drought conditions continued. A widespread soaking rain brought an inch to most areas between the 24th and 26th, with locally higher amounts upwards of 6 inches over the Midcoast and central Maine. This beneficial rainfall when combined with the end of the growing season lowered water demands for much of the region.  The U.S. drought monitor highlighted these positive trends with a slight reduction in moderate drought areas across central Maine.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The moderate (D1) drought conditions across Maine were reduced to a (D0) across portions of southern Oxford, Franklin, and Somerset Counties. Severe drought conditions remain unchanged for the central and northern portions of those counties as they received less rainfall. Between the beginning and end of September, the severe drought (D2) was reduced from 13.6% of the region to 7.5%. The cumulative area in drought (D0-D2) was reduced from 51% to 32% for the same period.  Rainfall deficits continued for the headwaters of the Penobscot, Kennebec, and Androscoggin Rivers, where D0 to D2 conditions remain.  Year to date deficits continued for the drought stricken Lakes Region with reports of  6 to 12 inches below normal since January 1. Short term recharge was observed over portions of the Kennebeck and Penobscot headwaters where reservoirs returned to near normal levels. Northern and western Maine reported near record low groundwater levels based on the USGS well stations in the area. Dry wells were reported in northern counties, notably in Oxford, Franklin, and Central Somerset Counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front moved from west to east across Maine on the evening of the 24th into the early morning of the 25th, bringing around an inch of rainfall to most of the area. The same frontal boundary lifted back into the state during the early morning hours of the 26th. The warm moist air returned and training showers and thunderstorms lingered over Mid-coast into central Maine. Rainfall rates increased to around 1.5 inches per hour and lingered for 2 to 3 hours. The total rainfall amounts were upwards of 5 to 6 inches between midnight and dawn. Localized flash flooding was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pittston's public works department reported a washout on Palmer Brook road. Flash flooding caused an overwhelmed culvert to undermine the roadway and take out both lanes.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cooper Rd is closed due to flooding from Hunts Meadow Rd. to Devine Rd in Whitefield, ME.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late season severe weather outbreak occurred across Maine as a cold front swept across the state during the afternoon peak heating.  A very moist and unstable airmass was in place across the region with highs reaching 80 degrees and dewpoints into the 60s.  This allowed for sufficient CAPE values to support damaging winds gusts and large hail as the cold front moved across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damaging outflow wind gusts from a severe thunderstorm downed multiple trees and telephone wires in Lebanon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A sharp cold front moved through Maine on the afternoon of September 6th.  Strong dynamics aloft with this early fall trough brought amble amounts of shear to the region. In addition, enough low level instability developed due to very steep lapse rates that allowed for a few storms to became severe with damaging wind gusts across the Mid-coast of Maine and the Kennebec River Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damaging outflow winds from a severe thunderstorm downed a large tree onto wires on route 201 in Topsham.
